Figure out how much it's going to cost to start and operate your business. So there's multiple factors to that. Starting a business is setting up shop. So let's say you want to start a clothing line. Well it sounds simple enough, you just want to make t-shirts and hats and sweatshirts and put a logo on it and that's it, right? Well there's a lot of things that you have to build and create in order to set up shop is what I call it. And then what do you need to operate that business afterwards? So let's go through the example. You want to start a t-shirt company. So the first thing you want to do is, I'll just use myself as an example, first thing you want to do is find a clothing manufacturer, find a web designer, find a t-shirt designer or a graphic designer that's going to do multiple products like hats, t-shirts, sweatshirts, pants, shoes, whatever it is that you like in the clothing space. Let's stick with t-shirts and hats to keep it simple. Well each of those people cost different amounts of money. The graphic designer, you're going to pay anywhere from $30 an hour to $200 an hour. With the average of $50 to $100 an hour. Now you have to figure how many lines or how many pieces will be in your line for the first season. Usually it's going to be about 12 pieces. So now to factor that in, it takes about three to six hours per piece. You're going to do 12 pieces. So that's 36 hours to 72 hours with the work. At, let's say it's around $100 an hour, that could cost you $7200 just for that part of the business. You then want to negotiate with that person and say, hey, I'll let you do my design my whole line, but I'll pay you $5,000 or $4,000 and see if they'll do it as a package deal or give them a few percent of the company. Next you have to figure out who's going to actually make it for you. If you notice in the back of every piece of clothing, there's a label. And on that label, it has an RN number. And the RN number is the warehouse that made the clothing for them, the actual factory that made it from scratch. You may not want to have to go through and figure all those things out, so you're going to want to find a factory or a manufacturer that can make that for you. Why I'm going through that example is the idea is so many people, hundreds of thousands of people try to start clothing lines year after year and so many fail because they don't realize there's so many parts designing the shirt, picking the right fabric, finding the right factory, making sure you build the website, the corporation, the trademark, all these business expenses that come along with it. But then you're going to be designing a new line every three to six months if you're going seasonally. Each time you do that, if it's 12 new pieces, assume it's $4,000 to $7,200 if you're getting them for that $100 an hour rate. And then factor that in four times because you're probably going to do it every three months to keep up seasonally. If your line's bigger and you want to have shorts and bracelets or necklaces, all of a sudden your cost expands. So you want to figure out your setup cost of design, legal, accounting, setting up shop, and figure out how many minimum orders you need when you go to a factory and you want to make t-shirts. Most of the time, unless you use some of the represent.com or cafepress.com or viralstyle.com or those types of sites where they'll print on demand for you, most of the time you're going to have to buy quantities. So you may have to get 144 pieces per style. If you have 12 styles, all of a sudden that's 12 times 144. You need nearly 2,000 pieces even at cost, even if you just get it for $5 to $6 a piece. That's still almost $12,000 in inventory just sitting in your garage or sitting in your closet. So maybe it doesn't sound like a lot, $10,000 to $12,000, but then you factored in that five grand for the design, 10 to 12 grand for the samples, a couple thousand dollars for the website. All of a sudden all these couple thousand dollar things, a couple thousand for legal, a couple thousand for accounting, you find yourself realizing you might need 30 to 100 thousand dollars just to set up shop. The good thing is if you go through those steps and you figure out how much you need ahead of time, those 2,000 pieces for example that are sitting in your garage, if you can then sell those pieces for $25 a unit on average, that $25 a unit times 20,000 pieces is $50,000. So if you're selling that much per quarter, $50,000 per quarter times four would be $200,000. You can now start to figure out your business model of if I make 2,000 pieces of clothing, I'm going to sell it for a $25 unit average. I'm going to earn $50,000 gross hopefully because you're praying that you sell out and also should factor in 100 units you need to give away to friends, retailers and samples, etc. You can start to figure out a business model. So the key takeaways are figure out each aspect of setting up your business, legal, accounting, design, building your site or building your app or whatever it is you're building, figure out all those core costs at the beginning before you start spending any money, figure out how much money you really need and then operating the business, do you need a full time designer, do you need a full time sales rep, do you need a full time manager, do you need a full time secretary, do you need a full time CEO, president, accountant, CFO, CTO, do you need an app designer, start figuring out what you need on a monthly basis, do you need a higher PR firm, do you need a full time legal firm. Each business, each industry needs different things on a monthly basis. So those recurring bills are very, very critical to figure out because even if they're small they add up. If something is only $1,500 a month, it sounds very inexpensive but $1,500 a month is $18,000 for the year. If there's a dozen expenses like that and a dozen times $18,000 you're now at $200,000 in expenses. I'll put something that was just $1,500 here, $1,500 there. So figuring out all those costs of what it takes to set up shop and then what you're expecting your needs are as an operating, an ongoing business are very, very critical to figure out at the very beginning.
